LAWS
OF THE
State of Kentucky
PASSED AT A SPECIAL SESSION OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
WHICH WAS BEGUN AND HELD IN THE CITY OF
FRANKFORT ON THURSDAY , THE TWELFTH
DAY OF JANUARY , NINETEEN
HUNDRED AND FIVE .
CHAPTER 1 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact section 1 of an act entitled , "An
Act to provide for the erection and completion of the Capitol and
other necessary buildings at the seat of Government ." Approved
by the Governor , February 6 , 1904 , and authorizing a change of
Capitol site, and provide for the acquisition of lands therefor.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That sectionone of an act,entitled, "An Act to
provide for the erectionand completion of the Capitol
and other necessary buildings at the seat of Govern-
ment ," approved February 6,1904,be amended by strik-
ing therefrom the last sentence of said section, which
sentence is in words as follows : “The buildings herein
provided forshallbe locatedand builtupon the grounds,
or lot, now used for the State Capitol, and not else-
where ," and inserting in lieu thereof, the following:
"The said board is hereby authorized, empowered and
directedto receiveas a gift,to purchase or condemn for
public use by writ of ad quod damnum , for the location
and erection of the State Capitol and other necessary

2 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
buildings, the following described tracts or parcels of
land, lying in and contiguous to the city of Frankfort,
Franklin County , Kentucky , and bounded as follows,
to-wit : First. That portion of property known as the
"Hunt Place ," which is situated at the Southern
terminiof Shelby, Main and Logan streets, in south
Frankfort,containingtwenty -two acresof ground bound-
ed by Shelby street and the property known as the
"Sayre" property on the south and southwest ; by the
Lawrenceburg turnpike, and the lot of the Frankfort
Water Company , and Mrs. Mary Johnson on the east,
and by the Frank Chinn residentproperty, and a vacant
lot on the north . Second . The vacant lot between
Logan streetand Main streetextended, fronting on the
latter about three hundred feet and running back to
Logan street about four hundred feet. Third . The
vacant lot between the residence of Frank Chinn and
the residence of William Cromwell , situated between
Main streetextended and Shelby streetfronting on the
latterabout one hundred and sixty-eight feet. Fourth .
The residentproperty of Frank Chinn , which is bound-
ed by the "Hunt " property on the south, by Main
street extended on the east ; by the above described
vacant loton the north and by Shelby streeton the west,
fronting on the latter about one hundred and sixty-
eight feet. Fifth. The lot of ground containing five
and one-fourthacres, conveyed by P. B. Hunt's executor
to Mary Johnson , and bounded by the present "Hunt "
property on the west and south, by the Frankfort
Water Works property and the Lawrenceburg turn-
pike on the east and on the north by the L. F. Johnson
property and the Frank Chinn property. Sixth. The
John W. Rodman lot, which frontssixty feeton Logan
street, and running back that width to Main street, ex-
tended about four hundred feet, and bounded by the
و

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 3
lotsof Chinn , Noel and Rodman on the north, and by
Chinn on the south. Seventh. A stripof ground about
fortyfeetwide,and running along the eastlineof Main
streetextended, from the south line of Todd streetto
the north line of the lot conveyed by Frank Chinn to
John W. Rodman ; Provided, That itbe left to the
discretionof said board as to whether or not they pur-
chase or receive the latter tract. Fighth . All the land
bounded on the west by lotof J. W. Rodman ,on the east
side of Main street extended, on the north by Todd
street,on the eastby Logan street, and on the south by
thelotofJ.W. Rodman ,hereinbeforementioned,which
land is severallyowned by J. C. Noel, Thomas Tanner ,
Mrs. Jane Buttimer, Frank Chinn and SallieHandy ;
Provided,That itbe leftto the discretionof said board
as to whether or not it purchases, receivesor procures
thislast-describedland and at a costnot exceedingtwen-
ty thousand dollars, and the said board is empowered to
receiveas a gift,to purchase or condemn for public use
by writ of ad quod damnum any land for a street or right
of way ,over or acrossany land which may intervenebe-
tween any streetor the Kentucky river in the city of
Frankfort and thelotor lotsofgrounds receivedby said
board ; said writ of ad quod damnum shallrun in the
name of the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund ,for the
use and benefitof the Commonwealth of Kentucky and
the proceedings to condemn any lands thereunder by the
terms of this act shallbe the same as the proceedings
now provided by law for the condemnation of land by
railroadsin thisState, and the said board is authorized
topay forsaidtractsor parcelsof land herein described
and all expenses necessary to obtain a perfect titleto
same , in the name of the Commonwealth of Kentucky ,
a sum not exceeding forty thousand dollars, and there is
hereby appropriatedthe sum of forty thousand dollars,

4 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
or so much thereof as may be necessary to make the
purchase of the firstseven described tracts of land ; and
in the event that said board,in itsdiscretion,shallpur-
chase , receive or procure the last-described lots of land ,
now owned by.J. C. Noel, Thomas Tanner , Mrs. Jane
Buttimer ,Frank Chinn and SallyHandy ,there ishereby
appropriated the additional sum of twenty thousand
dollars,or so much thereofas may be necessaryfor said
purpose. Said sum or sums of money to be paid out of
the treasury out of money not otherwise appropriated,
so that said section, when amended and re-enacted, will
read as follows :
"§ 1. That the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund
of Kentucky ,by that name and style,are hereby created
and authorized as a board to carry out the provisions of
this act, and erect and complete the buildings herein
provided for. The said board is hereby authorized ,
empowered and directedto receiveas a gift,to purchase
or condemn for public use by writ of ad quod damnum ,
for the location and erection of the State Capitol and
othernecessarybuildings,the followingdescribedtracts
or parcelsof land, lying in and contiguous to the city
of Frankfort, Franklin County ,Kentucky , and bounded
as follows, to-wit : First. That portion of property
known as the "Hunt Place " which is situated at the
southern termini of Shelby, Main and Logan streets, in
south Frankfort , containing twenty -two acres of ground ,
bounded by Shelby streetand the property, known as
the "Sayre" property on the south and the southwest ;
by the Lawrenceburg turnpike,and the lotof the Frank-
fortWater Company , and Mrs. Mary Johnson on the
east and by the Frank Chinn residentproperty and a
vacant lot on the north . Second . The vacant lot between
Logan streetand Main streetextended, frontingon the
latter about three hundred feet and running back to

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 5
Logan street about four hundred feet. Third .The vacant
lot between the residence of Frank Chinn and the resi-
dence ofWilliam Cromwell ,situatedbetween Main street
extended and Shelby street, fronting on the latter about
one hundred and sixty-eight feet. Fourth . The resi-
dent property of Frank Chinn , which isbounded by the
"Hunt " property on the south,by Main streetextended
on the east, by the above describedvacant lot on the
north, and by Shelby streeton the west,frontingon the
latterabout one hundred and sixty-eight feet. Fifth.
The lotof ground containing fiveand one-fourth acres,
conveyed by P. B. Hunt's executorto Mary L. Johnson ,
and bounded by the present "Hunt " property on the
west and south, by the Frankfort Water Works prop-
erty and the Lawrenceburg turnpike on the east, and on
the north by the L. F. Johnson property and the Frank
Chinn property . Sixth . The John W. Rodman lot
which fronts sixty feet on Logan street, and running
back that width to Main streetextended, about four
hundred feet and bounded by the lots of Chinn , Noel
and Rodman on the north, and by Chinn on the south.
Seventh . A strip of ground about forty feet wide , and
running along the eastlineof Main street,extended from
the south line of Todd street to the north line of the lot
conveyed by Frank Chinn to John W. Rodman ;
Provided,That itbe leftto the discretionof said board
as to whether or not they purchase or receivethe latter
tract. Eighth . All the land bounded on the west by
lot of J. W. Rodman , on the east side of Main street
extended, on the north by Todd street, on the east by
Logan streetand on the south by the lot of J. W. Rod-
man , hereinbeforementioned, which land is severally
owned by J. C. Noel, Thomas Tanner , Mrs. Jane Butti-
mer, Frank Chinn and SallyHandy ;Provided, That it
be left to the discretion of said board as to whether or

6 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
the
not itpurchases, receivesor procures this last-describ-
ed land, and at a cost not exceeding twenty thousand
dollars;and the said board isempowered to receiveas a
gift, to purchase, or condemn for public use by writ of
ad quod damnum any land for a street, or rightof way ,
over or across any land which may intervenebetween
any street or Kentucky river in the
city of Frankfort, and the lot or lots of
ground receivedby saidboard ;said writ of ad quod dam-
num shall run in the name of the the Commissioners of
the Sinking Fund and for the use and benefitof the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , and the proceedings to con-
demn any land thereunder by the terms of this act shall
be the same as the proceedings now provided by law
for the condemnation of land by railroads in this State ;
and the said board is authorizedto pay for said tracts
or parcels of land herein described, and all expenses
necessaryto obtain a perfecttitleto same , in the name
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , a sum not exceeding
fortythousand dollarsand thereishereby appropriated
the sum of forty thousand dollars, or so much thereof
as may be necessary to make the purchase of the first
seven -described tracts of land ; and in the event that
said board , in itsdiscretion, shallpurchase, receiveor
procure the last-describedlots of land, now owned by
J. C. Noel, Thomas Tanner ,Mrs. Jane Buttimer, Frank
Chinn and Sally Handy , there is hereby appropriated
the additionalsum of twenty thousand dollars, or so
much thereofas may be necessaryfor said purpose,said
sum or sums of money to be paid out of the treasuryout
of money not otherwise appropriated ."
§ 2. All acts and parts of acts in conflictwith this
act are hereby repealed to the extent of such conflict
only.
§ 3. Whereas the Governor has called the General

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 7
Assembly in extraordinarysessionto considerand act
upon thequestionofthechangeofcapitolsite,an emer-
gency is declaredto exist, and thisact shalltake effect
from and afteritspassageand approvalby the Gover-
nor .
Approved by the Governor February 14, 1905.

8 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
RESOLUTIONS .
No. 1 .
RESOLUTION for the benefit of the Ministers of the Frankfort
churches .
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
1. That there is hereby appropriated out of any
money in the Treasury not otherwise appropriated, the
sum of $75 for the benefit of the ministers who have
opened the sessionsof the Senate with prayer during
thepresentsession,alsoa likeamount forthe ministers
who have opened the House during the present session
with prayer ; said sums to be paid to the chief clerks of
the Senate and House ,to be distributedby them .
2. WHEREAS ,This money isjustlydue said ministers
and there is no law authorizingpayment of same , and
they need this assistance, this resolutionshall take ef-
fect upon its adoption .
Approved by the Governor February 13, 1905.
No. 2 .
RESOLUTION directing the payment to the widow of Hon . J. W.
McCain , deceased , amount which would have been due him during
this called session , had he lived .
WHEREAS , Hon . J. W. McCain , late Representative
from Trimble county, has sincethe adjournment of the
lastGeneral Assembly died, and ,

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 9
WHEREAS , It is the will of this House , the Senate
concurringtherein, that the per diem salarytogether
with railroadexpenses and stationeryisdue,and ought
to be paid to his surviving widow , therefore,
Be it resolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
wealth of Kentucky ,that the auditor of publicaccounts
be,and he isherebyauthorizedto draw hiswarrant on
the treasurerof the State, by the order of the clerk of
the House in favor of the widow of J. W. McCain , de-
ceased, the sum of money that would have been due her
'husband at theend of thepresentcalledsession,out of
money not otherwiseappropriated.
Became a law without the approval of the Governor, February 21,
1905 .
No. 3 .
RESOLUTION for the benefit of the pages , messengers , janitors,
doorkeepers , and cloakroom keepers of the House and Senate .
WHEREAS , The pages, messengers, janitors, door-
keepers and cloakroom -keepers of the Senate and
House , have performed theirdutieswith a fidelityand
industry that has greatly facilitatedthe labors of the
General Assembly at thisspecialsession, and ,
WHEREAS , Owing to the shortnessof the sessiontheir
per diem is insufficientto pay their board while here,
and theiractualtravelingexpenses in coming from and
returning to theirhomes , and
WHEREAS , It is not proper or just that they should
pay these expenses out of their slender means , now ,
therefore,

0 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
Be it resolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
wealth of Kentucky :
1. That the chief clerk of the Senate and the chief
clerkof the House , be authorizedand directedto draw
their warrants on the auditor of the State as follows :
The chief clerk of the Senate for the sum of ...........
dollars ($ ), and chiefclerkof the House for the
sum of ..dollars ($ ) and to dis-
tribute the said sums among the pages, messengers,
janitors, door-keepers, and cloakroom -keepers of their
respective Houses according as to the actual traveling
expenses of each may be,and the said clerkswillcertify
the same as contingent expenses of their respective
Houses .
Became a law without the approval of the Governor , February 21,
1905 .
No. 4 .
RESOLUTION for the benefit of Robert Lyons .
Be it resolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That the sum of ten dollars($10) ishereby appropri-
ated, out of any money in the treasury not otherwise
appropriated , to Robert Lyons , for services rendered in
waiting on committees of this General Assembly in
the court of appeals room , during this session of the
General Assembly .
Became a law without the approval of the Governor , February 21,
1905 .

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 11
No. 5 .
RESOLUTION for the benefit of Moses Butcher .
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
1. That Moses Butcher be allowed the sum of one
($1 ) dollarper day during this sessionof the General
Assembly , for servicein making firesand waiting on
committees,attendingand keeping firein legislativehall
rotunda, and cleaning water closetin legislativebuild-
ing.
2. Said servicesbeing now rendered,an emergency is
declared and this resolution shall be in force on its ap-
proval by the Governor .
Became a law without the approval of the Governor, February 21,
1905 .
No. 6 .
RESOLUTION for the benefit of certain employes of the House and
Senate .
WHEREAS , At the regular session of the General As-
sembly of Kentucky ,held in the year 1904, John Payne
was appointed as a messenger to the President of the
Senate, Leming B. Corlissas a specialmessenger to the
Senate and William Golden , as billclerk to the Senate ,
and ,
WHEREAS , Buford Osborne , Willie Beauchamp and
Paul Williams were by jointresolutionregularlyadopt-
ed, appointed as specialmessengers and billclerk to

12 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
the House , and J. Ekro Rapier, was appointed stenog-
rapher and assistant to the clerk, and ,
WHEREAS , Each of the above named persons, except
J. Ekro Rapier, has been present at thisextraordinary
sessionof the General Assembly, and performed the
dutiesincumbent upon them , and which they performed
at the regular session of the General Assembly , and ,
WHEREAS , J. Ekro Rapier resigned the position of
stenographer and assistantto the clerk, and the clerk
of the House with the approval of the speaker of the
House , under Rule 54, of the House , appointed to suc-
ceed J. Ekro Rapier, Miss A. D. Hutchison, therefore,
Be it resolved, by the House of Representatives, the
Senate concurringtherein,that the above named parties
be, and they are hereby appointed to the above several
named positionsfor thisextraordinaryterm of the Gen-
eralAssembly , and they are to receiveas pay for their
services,$2.50per day ,each, except Miss A. D. Hutchi-
✔son ,who shallreceive$3.00per day,and the auditorwill,
when a certifiedcopy of this resolution is presented to
him ,draw his warrant on the treasurerfor the amount
due each of said parties, and charge the aggregate
amounts to the contingent expense fund of the General
Assembly .
Became a law without the approval of the Governor , February 21,
1905 .
No. 7 .
RESOLUTION authorizing the employment of certain persons by the
custodian of public buildings and providing for their pay .
Be it resolved by the House of Representatives, the
Senate concurring therein, That Wm . Lyons , custodian
of public buildings, be and he is hereby authorized and

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 13
directed to employ such persons as may be necessary
to care for the back capitol, the stoves and furnaces
in capitolbuildings, and carryingbooks,etc., of clerks
of both Senate and House of Representatives to and
from theirofficeduring thissession. The pay of such
persons not to exceed in the aggregate four dollarsper
day ,the same to be approved and certifiedas contingent
expenses of this General Assembly by the chief clerks
of the Senate and House .
Became a law without the approval of the Governor, February 21,
1905 .
No. 8 .
RESOLUTION declaring the present capitol site unsuited for erec-
tion of the new capitol building . 1
Resolved by the House of Representatives, the Senate
concurring therein:
That it is the opinion of its membership , that the
present capitol site is inadequate, unsuited and un-
sightlyupon which to erectthe proposed new capitol.
No. 9 .
RESOLUTION of respect to the memory of Hon . L. E. Weatherford ,
deceased, formerly member of the Kentucky House of Represen-
tatives .
WHEREAS , On the third day of February , 1905, Hon .
L. E. Weatherford , of Graves County , Kentucky , de-
parted his life, and his spiritwas gathered to his
Creator,who gave it, and ,
WHEREAS , He was a member from Graves county, of
the lower House of the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth , for three successiveterms, therefore,

14 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That we , itsmembership, extend to his bereaved
family and relativesand friends our sinceresympathy
and condolence .
Resolved, Second, That the Statehas losta citizen
of sterlingworth and integrity, and that we fully ap-
preciate his most valiant services rendered to the State
while he was a member of the General Assembly, well
knowing that he had at all times the best interests of
his constituentsat heart, and never fearingto take a
stand for the right, or to oppose that which was wrong ,
Resolved, Third, That the law has lostone of itsmost
able advocates of the bar, and the State one of her hon-
ored sons,
Resolved, Fourth , That a copy of these resolutions
be spread upon the journal of each House , and a copy
sent to the bereaved family .
No. 10 .
RESOLUTION of respect to the memory of Hon . Carl Day , deceased,
late a member of the House of Representatives in the General
Assembly .
WHEREAS ,God the Divine Legislatorof allthings has
in his infinite wisdom seen fitto take from us our friend
and fellow worker, Honorable Carl Day , therefore,
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That by his death the Commonwealth has lostone of
itsmost energetic, conscientiousand hard working law
makers ; that we have lost a staunch, true and noble

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 15
friend, his constituentsone who was ever awake in
theirinterest, and untiring in his effortsto represent
the people ratherthan allow himself to be biasedby any
petty personal views ; his brother and sister alone sur-
viving him have lost a kind, indulgent and honored
brother, leaving two sorrowing hearts that time can
never heal,
Resolved,That the heartfeltsympathy of thisGeneral
Assembly ishereby tendered to hisbereaved and sorrow-
ing brother and sister;
Resolved, That a copy of this resolutionbe spread
at large upon the journal of the Senate and House of
Representatives,and that an engrossedcopy ofthe same ,
with the great seal of this Commonwealth appended
thereto,be presentedto his brother and sisteras an evi-
dence of thehigh regard and esteem in which he was held
by his colleaguesin the GeneralAssembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky .
No. 11 .
RESOLUTION of respect to the memory of Hon . Wm . C. P. Breckin-
ridge .
To the Senate and House of Representatives of Ken-
tucky :
We , the undersigned committee appointed to draft
suitable resolutions concerning the life and death of
Col. W. C. P. Breckinridge, would respectfullyreport
the following , and recommend its adoption :
Colonel William Cabell Preston Breckinridge fell
asleep and was gathered to the fathers on Saturday ,
November 19, 1904. A great man has fallenin Israel,
and a mighty leader is no more . Soldier, statesman,
orator,lawyer,thinker,publicist,leader,deeply devoted

16 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
1
to humanity ,loving Kentucky with the intensepassion
of a faithfulson, and our common country with the
dauntless devotion born of a prophetic vision of its
boundless possibilities; profoundly learned in all its
history of the past, deeply conscious of the pressing
duties and vexing problems of the day , with an ever
brightening faithand confidencein the unborn future,
he looked always upwards and forward, without dread
or doubt , with no misgivings .
His heart beat for humanity , and his voice and pen
spoke words of cheer,encouragement and hope,pointing
always to higher purpose and nobler ideal. Of daunt-
less courage, magnetic presence, broadly cultured, his
eloquence stirred the soul and moved the mind . Too
honest for sophistry, too strong for demagogy , too
clearminded for deceit,always and every where he was
the fearless and intrepid leader who followed the clear
lineof duty as he had lightto see it.
In the army following the flag of a doomed Con-
federacy, at the bar, and in the council chamber of a
common country, as soldier, lawyer, public man his
influencewas largeand potent,hisidealshigh and lofty,
and his intellectuality, dominant and transcendent.
Representing the best blood of many illustriousnames ,
he measured up to the full standard and added fade-
lessluster to the names he bore, therefore,
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That ,in hisdeath,the Commonwealth and the Nation
have lost a great man and a useful citizen who was
faithful unto death . Be it further,
Resolved, That these resolutionsbe spread at length
on the journals of the two Houses and that an enrolled
copy be sent to the family of the deceased.

LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 17
No. 12 .
RESOLUTION of respect to the memory of Hon . J. W. McCain , late
a member of the House of Representatives of Kentucky .
WHEREAS , Hon . J. W. McCain , late a Representative
of Trimble and Oldham counties in this General As-
sembly,has,sinceitslastregularsession, been removed
by death ,
Resolved, By the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , That by his death the General As-
sembly has losta wise,active,usefuland esteemed mem-
ber; his county and community a genial, patrioticand
loyal citizen; his widow and children a generous and
affectionatehusband and father, and his fellowsoldiers
in the Confederate cause, a brave and devoted comrade .
"The records of the General Assembly wherein are
registeredhis acts- notably the 'McCain Tobacco Bill,'
and other useful measures, testifyto the wisdom and
efficiencyof hisservicesin both houses of thisbody .
"To us,his fellowmembers , his absence causes heart-
feltgrief;to hisfamily,unfailingsorrow, and we and
they shallkeep his memory green.
" We bow in humble submission to this decree of the
All Wise . As He gave to the departed the boon and
the burden of life, so it was His to cut life'sbrittle
thread at the appointed time.
"We thereforecommit his memory to the keeping of
a people, gratefulfor hislifeand for his public services
-his body goes to its mother dust, 'as the dew drop
slipsinto the sea;' his immortal part we commit to the
Giver, truthfullyreflecting, that, though,
" We know not where God's Islands lift
Their fronded palms in air;
We only know we cannot drift,
Beyond his loving care .'

18 LAWS OF KENTUCKY .
"The heart-feltsympathy of the General Assembly
istendered to his bereaved family.
"Resolved, That these resolutionsbe spread at large
upon the journal of the House of Representatives, and
an engrossed copy of same be presented to his family as
a token of the respectof his colleaguesin the General
Assembly ."
